methods.description: Process for locating a fossil-bearing surface, dividing it into a grid, recording the spatial position and orientation of fossils, and collecting specimens.

steps.description: A surface layer of 9 × 3 m (27 m 2 ) yielding spatangoid echinoids was found in an artificial outcrop adjoining National Route 3.
steps.searchable_summary: Locate fossil-bearing geological layer in the field.

steps.description: The studied surface was divided into 12 quadrangles measuring 1.5 × 1.5 m (2.25 m 2 each) to record the position of the fossils in the field (Fig. 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Divide study area into grid cells (quadrangles).

steps.description: to record the position of the fossils in the field (Fig. 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Record spatial coordinates of fossils within quadrangles.

steps.description: Orientation of the anterior ambulacral groove was measured for 52 specimens preserved in life position (i.e. specimens still partially embedded in the sediment, almost complete, and oriented with their oral side downwards).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure anterior ambulacral groove orientation for in-situ specimens.

steps.description: Forty-five of them were collected in the field.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect in-situ fossil specimens.

steps.description: In addition, 15 loose specimens were collected as float from scree.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect loose fossil specimens from scree.

methods.description: Process for cleaning collected fossil specimens to remove adhering sediment and expose morphological features using manual tools, pneumatic tools, and sandblasting.

steps.description: Fossils were temporarily exported to the Natural History Museum of Vienna for cleaning and preparation. Temporary fossil export was authorized by Gobierno de la Provincia de Santa Cruz, Museo Argentino de Ciencias Naturales (IF-2018-58231802-APN-MACN#CONICET) and AFIP (Subrégimen de exportación temporaria sin modificación 18/073/ ET2/000311/V).
steps.searchable_summary: Arrange temporary export of collected fossils for preparation.

steps.description: Adhering sediment was removed manually using scalpels, needles, and chisels...
steps.searchable_summary: Clean fossils using manual tools to remove adhering sediment.

steps.description: ...as well as with pneumatic preparation tools, which also allowed removal of still attached spines that partly obscured relevant morphological features.
steps.searchable_summary: Clean fossils using pneumatic tools to remove sediment and spines.

steps.description: To remove the fine sediment deposited in the petals and the oral surface a RUBY® sandblaster using sodium carbonate powder with 50 μm grain size at 30 psi was employed.
steps.searchable_summary: Sandblast fossils with sodium carbonate powder to remove fine sediment.
inputs: Fossils with fine sediment in petals/oral surface
outputs: Fossils with fine sediment removed from petals and oral surface

steps.description: In addition, iron powder of 50 μm grain size was employed at 22 psi to clean the oral surface of specimens in which sediment was especially indurated.
steps.searchable_summary: Sandblast indurated oral surfaces with iron powder.
outputs: Fossils with indurated sediment removed from oral surface

methods.description: Measuring quantitative morphological characters of cleaned fossil specimens following a specific scheme.

steps.description: Specimens were measured with a digital caliper (error: 0.01), following the scheme employed by Stara et al. (2012) (Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure quantitative characters of fossils using digital caliper.

steps.description: following the scheme employed by Stara et al. (2012) (Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply a predefined scheme for selecting and performing measurements.

methods.description: Analyzing the spatial distribution and orientation of fossils on the studied surface using plotting, coordinate extraction, and statistical tests.

steps.description: Position of echinoids was plotted using Inkscape 0.92.3...
steps.searchable_summary: Create a plot of fossil positions.

steps.description: ...and exported into GeoGebra Classic 5.0.518.0-d (December 20, 2018) to extract the point coordinates.
steps.searchable_summary: Transfer spatial plot data to GeoGebra.

steps.description: ...to extract the point coordinates.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract precise coordinates from the spatial plot in GeoGebra.

steps.description: Poisson and Clark Evans tests were performed to identify clustering patterns of echinoids on the surface. Parameters were calculated based on 999 simulations of CSR with fixed n.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct Poisson test to assess spatial clustering.

steps.description: Poisson and Clark Evans tests were performed to identify clustering patterns of echinoids on the surface. Parameters were calculated based on 999 simulations of CSR with fixed n.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct Clark Evans test to assess spatial clustering.
outputs: Clark Evans test results (e.g., R value, p-value)

steps.description: Kernel smoothed density was calculated and plotted to observe aggregation patterns.
steps.searchable_summary: Compute kernel density estimate of fossil distribution.

steps.description: Kernel smoothed density was calculated and plotted to observe aggregation patterns.
steps.searchable_summary: Visualize the kernel density estimate.

steps.description: Furthermore, to determine if the fossils had any preferential orientation in the field, a rose diagram and a Rayleigh Test for preferred orientation was made.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate a rose diagram to visualize fossil orientations.

steps.description: Furthermore, to determine if the fossils had any preferential orientation in the field, a rose diagram and a Rayleigh Test for preferred orientation was made.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct Rayleigh test to assess if orientations are non-uniform (preferred).
outputs: Rayleigh test results (e.g., Z statistic, p-value)

methods.method_name: Morphological Comparison using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)
methods.description: Comparing morphometric measurements of collected specimens with previously published data using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to assess species distinctiveness.

steps.description: To test morphological differences with the specimens published by Martínez and del Río (2017) and Carmona et al. (2020), a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) was performed, including the morphometric measurements of all the echinoids mentioned in those publications.
steps.searchable_summary: Combine morphometric data from different sources for analysis.
inputs: Morphometric data from Martínez and del Río (2017)
Morphometric data from Carmona et al. (2020)
outputs: Combined morphometric dataset with group labels (e.g., source publication/collection)

steps.description: To test morphological differences with the specimens published by Martínez and del Río (2017) and Carmona et al. (2020), a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) was performed...
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to the combined morphometric dataset.

steps.description: The LDA finds the component axes that maximize the separation between multiple classes (Pérez López, 2004). In this case, the purpose is to find if all the specimens considered to be Brisaster iheringi in Patagonia belong to the same species or not, using morphometric data.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret LDA results to determine separation between groups and assess species distinctiveness.
inputs: LDA model results (e.g., separation scores, plots)
outputs: Assessment of morphological distinctiveness between groups
Conclusion regarding species identity based on morphometry

methods.description: Preparation and microscopic analysis of thin sections from fossil material to study taphonomic and mineralogical features.

steps.description: Two thin sections of supraambital plates from interambulacrum 2 of one specimen allowed an analysis of taphonomic features.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare thin sections from fossil material.
inputs: Fossil material (supraambital plates from one specimen)

steps.description: Mineralogical features were observed with Zeiss® Discovery V20 and Nikon® Eclipse E200 Microscopes using normal and polarized light.
steps.searchable_summary: Examine thin sections using microscopes under normal and polarized light.

methods.description: Processing bulk sediment samples collected in the field to investigate microfauna content by disaggregation, drying, and sieving.

steps.description: Five bulk samples were taken in the field in order to investigate the microfauna of the sediments containing the spatangoid assemblage studied.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect bulk sediment samples in the field.

steps.description: After drying, the bulk sample was disaggregated with 10% hydrogen peroxide...
steps.searchable_summary: Dry the bulk sediment samples.

steps.description: ...the bulk sample was disaggregated with 10% hydrogen peroxide...
steps.searchable_summary: Disaggregate sediment using hydrogen peroxide.

steps.description: ...dried at 40 • C...
steps.searchable_summary: Dry disaggregated sediment at 40°C.

steps.description: ...and sieved into 250, 125 and 63 μm fractions. The other four sediment samples were treated in the same way and sieved into 2000, 1000, 500, 250, 125, 62, and <62 μm fractions.
steps.searchable_summary: Sieve dried, disaggregated sediment into multiple size fractions.

methods.description: Documenting specimens and features using various cameras and microscopes.

steps.description: Photographs were taken using a Zeiss® AxioCam Mrc 5 (attached to a Zeiss Stereo Discovery V20 Microscope), Nikon® D5200, and Nikon®
steps.searchable_summary: Capture images of specimens and features using specified equipment.
